"Chinese firms have long sought a foothold in this market. In 2016, Alibaba formed a joint venture with the Saudi Cloud Computing Company. In 2025, Alibaba opened a new data center in Dubai. Huawei expanded its Gulf presence through domestic telecom partnerships and scaled its Riyadh cloud hub in 2023. Tencent is the latest entrant. While it started building its first data center in Bahrain in 2021, it scaled up cloud infrastructure in 2025 with a $150 million Saudi investment. At the same time, competition in cloud infrastructure in the Gulf has become far more crowded than a simple U.S.-China battle. Gulf countries have increasingly invested in their own data center infrastructure, reducing reliance on external providers. Across the region, telecom operators and state-backed firms are building local capacity. Saudi Arabia and the UAE are rapidly repositioning themselves as global AI hubs, investing tens of billions of dollars in data centers, smart cities, and digital infrastructure. Saudi Arabia expects AI to contribute around 12% of GDP by 2030, while the UAE aims to generate roughly $96 billion from AI over the same period. The region’s data center market is projected to nearly triple to around $9.5 billion by 2030. Qatar’s Ooredoo has spun off its data center arm, Syntys, which operates facilities across multiple countries and recently expanded in Doha. Saudi Arabia’s STC and Kuwait’s Zain are also expanding colocation infrastructure to attract enterprise and hyperscaler demand. Even if Chinese cloud providers benefit in the near term, they could only gain traction as “secondary or backup options, particularly for non-critical workloads and regional redundancy,” Manish Ranjan, research director for software and cloud at IDC, told Rest of World." https:// restofworld.org/2026/huawei-ch ina-cloud-gulf-resilience-aws-strikes/ # MiddleEast # Gulf # China # AI # AWS # Huawei # DataCenters

The Irony of AI Fact Checking 🌍 The Global Power Triangle: Energy • Trade • AI Let’s turn your idea into a clear geopolitical diagram in words —this is how power is structured today and where it’s heading: 🔺 1. ENERGY (The Foundation Layer) Who controls fuel = controls movement Oil & Gas (Middle East, especially Strait of Hormuz) LNG (Qatar, UAE) Electricity generation (coal, nuclear, renewables) 👉 This layer powers: Ships Factories Cities Data centers Key truth: Without energy, nothing moves—not even AI. 🚢 2. TRADE ROUTES (The Circulatory System) Who controls movement = controls economies Strait of Hormuz → oil chokepoint Suez Canal → Europe–Asia link Indian Ocean routes → Africa–Asia trade Ports (Dubai, Singapore, Rotterdam) 👉 This layer moves: Fuel Food Minerals Manufactured goods Key truth: Trade routes are like arteries of the world economy . 🧠 3. AI & DIGITAL INFRASTRUCTURE (The Brain) Who controls data = controls decisions Data centers (US, Europe, Gulf, China) Submarine internet cables AI systems (cloud, compute, chips) 👉 This layer controls: Financial systems Logistics optimization Border systems & migration tech Military intelligence Key truth: AI doesn’t just use energy—it directs how energy and trade are used . ⚡ HOW THEY CONNECT (Your Core Insight) 1. Energy → Powers AI Data centers need massive electricity Future = nuclear, fusion, renewables 2. Trade Routes → Feed AI Chips, servers, rare earth minerals move via shipping Supply chains = physical backbone of digital systems 3. AI → Optimizes & Controls Both Predicts shipping routes Manages energy grids Influences geopolitics and policy 🧭 THE NEW POWER MODEL Think of it like this: 🧠 AI (Control Layer) ▲ │ 🚢 Trade ───┼─── Energy ⚡ (Movement) (Power) Energy gives power Trade gives reach AI gives control 👉 Whoever integrates all three becomes a super-system power 🌍 WHERE AFRICA (AfCFTA) FITS — IMPORTANT This connects directly to your thinking: Africa can position itself as: Energy hub (solar, green hydrogen) Trade corridor (AfCFTA, Lobito Corridor, ports) Digital gateway (data centers, digital borders) 👉 If aligned properly: Africa doesn’t just participate — it becomes a bridge between systems 🔥 FINAL STRATEGIC INSIGHT The future is not about: Oil alone AI alone Trade alone It is about: Integration of energy, logistics, and intelligence into one system 1. Is Sam Altman operating in the Strait of Hormuz? There is no evidence that Sam Altman is directly operating shipping routes, factories, or plantations in the Strait of Hormuz . What is true : He is involved in AI infrastructure expansion globally , including the Middle East (e.g. UAE partnerships and data center ambitions) ( The New Yorker ) He supports technology partnerships with Gulf nations ( Business Insider ) His focus is on AI, energy (fusion), and computing infrastructure , not agriculture or maritime logistics ( The Deep View ) 👉 So: Altman = digital infrastructure (AI, data, energy) Not = plantations, shipping chokepoints, or physical trade routes 2. What actually exists around the Strait of Hormuz? The Strait of Hormuz is one of the most strategic corridors on Earth. Key reality: ~20% of global oil passes through it Huge volumes of gas (LNG) and fertilizer inputs move through it ( Al Habtoor Research Centre ) 3. Real industries (factories & production systems) 🛢️ Energy & Industrial Infrastructure Operated by sovereign nations like: Qatar → LNG plants (QatarEnergy) Saudi Arabia → oil refining, petrochemicals United Arab Emirates → energy + logistics hubs Iran → النفط (oil), الغاز (gas), and terminals 👉 These are state-controlled or national companies , not private tech CEOs. 🌾 Fertilizer & Agro-industrial Chains The region exports: Urea Ammonia Sulfur-based inputs Nearly half of global fertilizer supply chains are linked to this region ( Sott.net ) 👉 These are industrial plants , not “plantations” in the traditional farming sense. 🚢 Logistics & Maritime Trade Systems Massive tanker fleets Strategic ports (e.g. UAE logistics hubs) Insurance, shipping, naval protection systems 4. Are there “plantations” in these countries? Not in the classical sense like Africa or Latin America. Instead: Gulf countries rely heavily on imports Limited agriculture due to desert climate Some controlled agriculture (greenhouses, desalination farming) 5. The deeper truth (important) What you’re sensing is correct in a broader sense , but needs clarity: The Strait of Hormuz is about: Energy control Industrial inputs Global supply chains People like Sam Altman are trying to build: AI infrastructure Energy systems (fusion, electricity for data centers) 👉 These two worlds are beginning to intersect , but they are not the same system . Final insight Hormuz = physical power (oil, gas, fertilizer, shipping) Altman = digital power (AI, compute, energy demand) The future conflict or cooperation is not about plantations—it’s about: Who controls energy, computation, and the arteries of global trade at the same time. #china #iran #middleEast #news #politics

This marks the first direct Iranian attack on Israeli territory since the beginning of the war in Gaza on October 7, 2023. The attacks involved a combination of ballistic and cruise missiles, with Iranian state media reporting that over 100 missiles and drones were launched. Israeli air defenses, aided by the United States, United Kingdom, France, Jordan, and Bahrain, intercepted the vast majority of the incoming projectiles. Israeli officials reported limited damage and no casualties, although some minor injuries were sustained. The IRGC’s attack was preceded by a public announcement from Iran indicating its intent to retaliate for the Damascus strike. This announcement served as a warning to Israel and the international community, allowing them time to prepare for the response. The timing of the attack, occurring on a Saturday night in Israel, likely aimed to maximize impact and minimize civilian casualties. The Damascus airstrike, attributed by Western media to Israel, significantly escalated tensions between Iran and Israel. The IRGC officers killed were reportedly involved in coordinating Iranian support for Hezbollah and other proxy groups operating in Lebanon and Syria. Iran has consistently sought to expand its influence in the region through these proxy forces, challenging U.S. and Israeli interests. Following the missile attacks, Iran has signaled a willingness to engage in diplomatic efforts to de-escalate the situation, although it has also warned of further action if Israel responds. The United States and its allies have condemned Iran’s attacks and reiterated their support for Israel’s right to defend itself. The international community is now focused on preventing further escalation and establishing a sustainable path towards de-escalation. The attack raises concerns about a wider regional conflict. Iran’s decision to directly attack Israel, rather than relying on proxy groups, marks a significant shift in its strategy and demonstrates its willingness to confront its adversaries directly.
